notes:
OWASP produces multiple weakness taxonomies for different domains, from traditional web security to AI/ML systems. OWASP's Top 10 lists are the most widely recognized weakness taxonomies in the industry. Practitioner-focused, regularly updated, community-driven, and free/open (no licensing restrictions).

Evolution of AI coverage: OWASP Top 10 (Web, 2003→2021), LLM Top 10 v1.0 (2023), ML Top 10 (2023), AI Exchange (2024), LLM Top 10 v2.0 (2025), Agentic AI Top 10 (2025).

data.description: The most critical web application security risks. Updated every 3-4 years.
data.notes:
Version matters: top10@2021#A01 (Broken Access Control) is not the same as top10@2017#A1 (Injection). ID format differs by version: 2021 uses zero-padded IDs (A01-A10), while 2017 and 2013 use non-padded IDs (A1-A10). The lookup URL requires assembling the item number, year, and name slug — not a simple {id} substitution, so each version uses a lookup_table. Maps to CWEs.

2013 individual pages are no longer live — only a PDF of the full document is available. 2017 URLs have a quirk: A10's URL contains %2526 (double-encoded ampersand) because the title 'Insufficient Logging & Monitoring' has & encoded as %26, then % encoded as %25.
data.version_disambiguation: Versions are released by year. Match the version whose release year is closest to but not after the referring document's publication date. If no date context is available, use the latest version (2021). Note: item numbering restarts with each version — A01 in one version is unrelated to A01 in another.

versions_available.note: Major restructuring from 2017. A01 changed from Injection to Broken Access Control.
Still widely referenced in existing documentation and certifications.
Legacy. Occasionally referenced in older compliance documentation.

data.note: Major restructuring from 2017. A01 changed from Injection to Broken Access Control. Uses zero-padded IDs (A01-A10).

provenance.method: Verified URLs at owasp.org/Top10/ listing page. Each URL confirmed to load.

examples.note: Broken Access Control — #1 in 2021, was #5 in 2017
Injection — was #1 in 2017, dropped to #3 in 2021

data.note: Still widely referenced in existing documentation and certifications. Uses non-padded IDs (A1-A10, NOT A01-A10). A10 URL contains double-encoded ampersand (%2526).

provenance.method: Verified URLs at owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/2017/ listing page. A10 URL confirmed to use double-encoded %2526 for the & character.

examples.note: Non-padded ID format (A1 not A01). Injection was #1 in 2017.
URL contains double-encoded ampersand (%2526) for the & in 'Logging & Monitoring'

data.note: Legacy. Individual item pages are no longer live. Only the full PDF document is available. Uses non-padded IDs (A1-A10).

urls.note: Full document PDF. Individual item web pages no longer available.

data.note: No individual item URLs available. Return the PDF link from the parent version node.

data.description: Security risks specific to Large Language Model applications.
data.notes: AI/ML-specific weakness categories. Maps to CWE and MITRE ATLAS. Updated more frequently than traditional OWASP Top 10. Item numbering is reused across versions but items were significantly renamed and reordered between v1.0 and v2.0. Actual titles from OWASP may differ from commonly cited names (e.g., 'Improper Output Handling' not 'Insecure Output Handling' for v2.0 LLM05).
data.version_disambiguation: v2.0 (2025) significantly restructured from v1.0 (2023). Match the version whose release date is closest to but not after the referring document's publication date. If no date context, use v2.0. Item numbering is reused — LLM01 in v2.0 is the same topic (Prompt Injection) but with substantially updated content; other items changed more significantly.

versions_available.note: Major update. Labelled '2025' in titles (e.g., 'LLM01:2025'). Some items renamed and reordered from v1.0.
First edition. Content archived at different URL path.

data.note: Major update from v1.0. Labelled '2025' in titles (e.g., 'LLM01:2025'). Some items renamed and reordered. LLM01 URL slug lacks the '2025' year that LLM02-10 have — confirmed this is how OWASP published it, not a data entry error.

provenance.method: Searched genai.owasp.org for 'site:genai.owasp.org/llmrisk/' to find all v2.0 (2025) URLs, then verified each URL loads. LLM01 slug lacks the '2025' year that all other entries have (llm01-prompt-injection vs llm022025-...) — confirmed this is how OWASP published it, not a data entry error.

examples.note: LLM01 URL slug lacks '2025' year — unlike LLM02-10. This is how OWASP published it.

data.note: First edition (October 2023). Archived. URL slugs use inconsistent casing (CamelCase, snake_case, abbreviation) — that is how OWASP published them, not a data entry error. Page headings are just titles without LLM ID prefixes.

provenance.method: Found v1.0 items at Archive/1_0_vulns/ path (NOT 0_1_vulns which is v0.1). URL slugs verified against GitHub repository file listing. Inconsistent slug casing (CamelCase vs snake_case) matches OWASP's published filenames.

examples.note: Snake_case slug (inconsistent with LLM01 CamelCase — matches OWASP's published filenames)

data.description: Security risks specific to Machine Learning systems — broader than LLMs, covering vision, classification, and other ML domains.
data.notes: Focuses on traditional ML security (not just LLMs). Covers attacks at both training and inference time. Complements the LLM Top 10 for non-LLM systems. More technical/research oriented vs LLM Top 10's deployment/integration focus.

urls.note: Uses URL hash fragments. The page is a single-page site with anchored sections — navigate within the page to find the matching item. Automated resolution should note that the # in the URL is a fragment identifier, not a subpath delimiter.

children.description: ML Top 10 item number (e.g., ML01, ML05)

data.note: URL uses hash fragment — this is a single-page site with anchored sections, not separate pages per item.

examples.note: URL uses hash fragment on single-page site

data.description: Security risks specific to AI agents — autonomous systems that can plan, act, use tools, and make decisions with limited human oversight.
data.notes:
Released December 10, 2025. Developed by 100+ industry experts. Agentic systems introduce unique risks not covered by LLM Top 10: autonomy (agents act without per-action human approval), tool use (code execution, API calls, file modification), chaining (multi-agent collaboration amplifying risks), persistence (state across sessions), and goal pursuit (unexpected achievement paths). Key principle: Least Agency — only grant agents the minimum autonomy required. Critical for MCP, LangChain, AutoGPT, CrewAI security. Complements LLM Top 10, doesn't replace it.

Supporting resources: State of Agentic Security 1.0, Agentic Security Solutions Landscape, Practical Guide to Securing Agentic Applications, OWASP FinBot CTF.

versions_available.note: First edition. Released December 2025, labelled '2026'. Developed by 100+ industry experts.

children.description: Agentic Top 10 item number (e.g., ASI01, ASI06)

data.description: Comprehensive AI security knowledge base with a 'Periodic Table of AI Security' organizing threats and controls.
data.notes: The lookup URL https://owaspai.org/goto/{id}/ redirects to the detailed page for each threat or control. IDs are uppercase concatenated words (e.g., DIRECTPROMPTINJECTION). Maps to MITRE ATLAS, CWE, and other frameworks. Continuously updated with emerging threats. Each threat maps to controls in secid:control/owasp.org/ai-exchange.

children.description: AI Exchange threat/control ID — uppercase concatenated words

data.description: AI-specific vulnerability scoring framework extending CVSS with metrics for model robustness, data sensitivity, ethical impact, and other AI-specific risk factors.
data.notes:
v0.5 published; v1.0 targeted for RSA Conference late March 2026. Traditional CVSS doesn't capture AI-specific risk factors: probabilistic attack success (vs binary exploitability), adaptive attacks (vs static attack vectors), cascading AI failures (vs single impact type), and model-update-dependent vulnerabilities (vs fixed temporal metrics).

Two scoring frameworks coexist in v0.5: (1) 'Classic AIVSS' with 5 CVSS-derived Base metrics + 5 AI-Specific metrics + 4 Impact metrics + 3 Temporal metrics + 4 Environmental metrics, using a weighted product formula; (2) 'Agentic AIVSS v0.5' with 10 amplification factors and a simpler formula (CVSS_Base + AARS) / 2 x ThM. The classic metrics have formal abbreviations suitable for subpath references; the Agentic amplification factors do not yet have formal abbreviations.

Metric abbreviations below are from the classic framework. Base metrics (AV, AC, PR, UI, S) are inherited from CVSS. AI-Specific metrics (MR, DS, EI, DC, AD) are AIVSS-original. Impact metrics (C, I, A, SI) extend CVSS with Societal Impact. Temporal (E, RL, RC) and Environmental (CR, IR, AR, SIR) are CVSS-derived.

versions_available.note: First published version. Introduces both classic multi-metric and Agentic scoring frameworks.

children.description: AIVSS metric abbreviation (e.g., AV, MR, DS, SI)

data.note: Metric abbreviations from the classic AIVSS framework. Each metric belongs to one of five groups: Base (CVSS-derived), AI-Specific (AIVSS-original), Impact, Temporal, or Environmental. Values are scored on group-specific scales.

examples.note: Model Robustness — AI-Specific metric (AIVSS-original, no CVSS equivalent)
Societal Impact — AIVSS-original extension to the CIA triad

known_values.AV: Attack Vector (Base) — Network/Adjacent/Local/Physical. How the vulnerability is exploited.
known_values.AC: Attack Complexity (Base) — Low/High. Conditions beyond attacker control required for exploitation.
known_values.PR: Privileges Required (Base) — None/Low/High. Level of access needed before exploitation.
known_values.UI: User Interaction (Base) — None/Required. Whether a human must participate for exploitation.
known_values.S: Scope (Base) — Unchanged/Changed. Whether exploitation impacts resources beyond the vulnerable component.
known_values.MR: Model Robustness (AI-Specific) — Resilience to adversarial attacks: evasion resistance, gradient masking, robustness certification.
known_values.DS: Data Sensitivity (AI-Specific) — Data confidentiality, integrity, and provenance risks.
known_values.EI: Ethical Impact (AI-Specific) — Bias, transparency, accountability, and societal impact concerns.
known_values.DC: Decision Criticality (AI-Specific) — Consequences of incorrect AI decisions: safety-critical, financial, reputational, operational.
known_values.AD: Adaptability (AI-Specific) — Security maintenance over time: monitoring, retraining, threat intelligence, adversarial training.
known_values.C: Confidentiality Impact (Impact) — None/Low/Medium/High/Critical. Impact on data confidentiality.
known_values.I: Integrity Impact (Impact) — None/Low/Medium/High/Critical. Impact on data and system integrity.
known_values.A: Availability Impact (Impact) — None/Low/Medium/High/Critical. Impact on system availability.
known_values.SI: Societal Impact (Impact) — None/Low/Medium/High/Critical. Impact on society or safety. AIVSS-original extension to CVSS impact triad.
known_values.E: Exploitability (Temporal) — Not Defined/Unproven/Proof-of-Concept/Functional/High. Maturity of exploit techniques.
known_values.RL: Remediation Level (Temporal) — Not Defined/Official Fix/Temporary Fix/Workaround/Unavailable.
known_values.CR: Confidentiality Requirement (Environmental) — Not Defined/Low/Medium/High. Adjusts Confidentiality Impact based on deployment context.
known_values.IR: Integrity Requirement (Environmental) — Not Defined/Low/Medium/High. Adjusts Integrity Impact based on deployment context.
known_values.AR: Availability Requirement (Environmental) — Not Defined/Low/Medium/High. Adjusts Availability Impact based on deployment context.
known_values.SIR: Societal Impact Requirement (Environmental) — Not Defined/Low/Medium/High. AIVSS-original extension adjusting Societal Impact based on deployment context.
